content.assessment: Fiscal conditionality is notable as a governance strategy explicitly designed to avoid the centralized-admissions-versus-free-choice legal tension, by operating through funding levers rather than admissions mandates. This gives it a distinct legal-authority profile: it may not require amending Act CXC's admissions provisions at all, though it would require new fiscal-regulatory authority to condition per-pupil premiums on equity outcomes, and enforceability against maintainer autonomy remains untested.
opportunities: A funding-based lever could be more legally durable than admissions mandates precisely because it avoids direct conflict with the free-choice/centralized-exam tension
strengths: Explicitly designed to sidestep the legal tension between centralized admissions gates and statutory free-choice guarantees by acting through funding rather than admissions criteria [F-live-legal_and_governance-02]
threats: Maintainers could respond by adjusting non-SES criteria, creating new accountability and rights-compatibility problems the proposal does not anticipate
weaknesses: No direct evidence in the record establishes fiscal conditionality's causal effect on segregation, leaving the accountability mechanism speculative

Church and private maintainers' independence from Klebelsberg Centre governance may extend to resistance against fiscal conditionality if it is perceived as a backdoor admissions mandate [F-live-legal_and_governance-03]
